observation_en: The solution warms slightly and becomes clear as the acid and base neutralize each other, forming salt water.
explanation_en: HCl donates a proton (H⁺) to OH⁻ from NaOH, forming water. The Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions remain dissolved as NaCl. This is an exothermic reaction that releases heat.
condition_notes: Occurs readily at room temperature. No catalyst needed. Ensure equimolar amounts for complete neutralization.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: Vigorous fizzing and bubbling as CO₂ gas is released. The mixture foams up noticeably.
explanation_en: Acetic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate in a double displacement reaction. The carbonic acid intermediate immediately decomposes into CO₂ gas and water.
condition_notes: Proceeds rapidly at room temperature. Acidic medium required. Use a large container to contain the foam.
safety_notes: Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: A reddish-brown rust (iron oxide) gradually forms on the iron surface over time.
explanation_en: Iron is oxidized by oxygen in the presence of moisture. Fe loses electrons (oxidation) while O₂ gains electrons (reduction), forming Fe₂O₃. This is a slow redox process accelerated by water and salt.
condition_notes: Very slow at room temperature. Moisture accelerates the reaction. A catalyst can lower the effective activation energy.

warnings_en: Do not look directly at the flame.

observation_en: Brilliant white flame with intense light. A white powdery ash (MgO) remains after combustion.
explanation_en: Magnesium burns in oxygen with a very bright white flame. The Mg atoms are oxidized to Mg²⁺ ions, forming magnesium oxide. This is a highly exothermic combustion reaction.
condition_notes: Requires high ignition temperature (~473°C). Once started, burns vigorously. Do NOT look directly at the flame — UV radiation hazard.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

observation_en: Zinc dissolves with vigorous bubbling as hydrogen gas is evolved. The solution becomes warm.
explanation_en: Zinc is more reactive than hydrogen in the activity series. Zn atoms are oxidized to Zn²⁺, displacing H⁺ ions which combine to form H₂ gas. This is a single displacement reaction.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium required. Hydrogen gas is flammable — keep away from flames.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: A white curdy precipitate of AgCl forms immediately upon mixing the two clear solutions.
explanation_en: Silver ions (Ag⁺) combine with chloride ions (Cl⁻) to form insoluble silver chloride (AgCl). The other ions (Na⁺, NO₃⁻) remain in solution. This follows solubility rules for halides.
condition_notes: Instant at room temperature. AgCl is photosensitive — darkens when exposed to light.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Concentrated hydrogen peroxide is a strong oxidizer.

observation_en: Rapid bubbling and foaming as oxygen gas is released. MnO₂ remains unchanged — it acts as a catalyst.
explanation_en: MnO₂ lowers the activation energy for H₂O₂ decomposition. The peroxide breaks down into water and oxygen gas. The catalyst is recovered unchanged at the end of the reaction.
condition_notes: Catalyst significantly lowers the threshold temperature. Without MnO₂, decomposition is very slow.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: Do not touch sodium with bare hands.

observation_en: Sodium darts across the water surface, producing hydrogen gas, heat, and sometimes sparks or flames. The solution becomes strongly basic.
explanation_en: Sodium is highly reactive (Group 1 alkali metal). It reduces water, releasing H₂ gas and forming NaOH. The reaction is violently exothermic and can ignite the hydrogen produced.
condition_notes: Reacts instantly at any temperature. Extremely exothermic. Use tiny pieces only. Keep away from flammable materials.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: Work in a ventilated area due to CO₂ gas.

observation_en: Vigorous effervescence as CO₂ gas is released. The calcium carbonate dissolves as the reaction proceeds.
explanation_en: HCl attacks the carbonate ion, releasing CO₂ gas and water. The calcium ions remain in solution as CaCl₂. This reaction is used to test for carbonates in geology.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Grinding the CaCO₃ increases surface area and reaction rate.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: Potassium permanganate is a strong oxidizer.
Oxygen may evolve rapidly with fizzing.

observation_en: The deep purple permanganate solution decolorizes to a pale, nearly colorless manganese(II) solution. Oxygen bubbles are released.
explanation_en: MnO₄⁻ (Mn⁷⁺) is reduced to Mn²⁺ by hydrogen peroxide in acidic conditions. The purple color disappears as Mn changes oxidation state from +7 to +2. H₂O₂ acts as both oxidizer and reducer here.
condition_notes: Acidic medium essential (H₂SO₄). Add peroxide slowly. The decolorization rate serves as a measure of reaction kinetics.
safety_notes: Oxidizer — do not mix with combustibles. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: Handle acids and iron salts with care.
Avoid direct contact with skin and eyes.

observation_en: A vivid blood-red color develops instantly as Fe³⁺ ions form a complex with SCN⁻ ions.
explanation_en: Fe²⁺ is first oxidized to Fe³⁺ by H₂O₂, then Fe³⁺ coordinates with SCN⁻ to form [Fe(SCN)]²⁺, an intensely blood-red complex. This is a classic qualitative test for iron(III) ions.
condition_notes: Acidic medium stabilizes the complex. Room temperature. The color intensity is proportional to Fe³⁺ concentration (Beer-Lambert law).
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

name_en: Aluminum with Sodium Hydroxide in Water

warnings_en: Use good ventilation and keep away from sparks.

observation_en: Aluminum dissolves with vigorous bubbling. Hydrogen gas escapes and the solution becomes warm.
explanation_en: Aluminum is amphoteric — it reacts with strong bases. NaOH dissolves the Al₂O₃ protective layer, allowing Al to react with water. Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂) forms in solution.
condition_notes: Basic medium required. Slightly above room temperature. Exothermic — hydrogen evolved is flammable.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Intense heat is released. Steam may be produced. The mixture swells and crumbles into a white powder (slaked lime).
explanation_en: Quicklime (CaO) reacts exothermically with water to form slaked lime — calcium hydroxide Ca(OH)₂. The reaction can raise temperatures above 100°C and is used in self-heating food packages.
condition_notes: Add water gradually to the lime, not the reverse. Extremely exothermic. The product is a strong base.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns.

observation_en: A blue gelatinous precipitate of copper(II) hydroxide forms immediately in the mixed solution.
explanation_en: Cu²⁺ ions from CuSO₄ combine with OH⁻ ions from NaOH to form insoluble Cu(OH)₂. This is a double displacement reaction governed by solubility rules.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Basic medium. The blue precipitate can be filtered and dried.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: A brilliant yellow precipitate of lead(II) iodide (PbI₂) forms — the 'golden rain' reaction when dissolved in hot water and slowly cooled.
explanation_en: Pb²⁺ from lead nitrate combines with I⁻ from potassium iodide to form PbI₂, which is insoluble at room temperature. When heated and cooled, PbI₂ recrystallizes as golden flakes.
condition_notes: Room temperature for precipitation. Heat and slow cool for the 'golden rain' crystal demonstration.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk.

name_en: Barium Chloride and Sodium Sulfate (Sulfate Test)

warnings_en: Barium chloride is toxic if ingested.

observation_en: A dense white precipitate of barium sulfate forms immediately. It is insoluble in acids and water.
explanation_en: Ba²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ combine to form BaSO₄, one of the most insoluble salts known. This reaction is the standard qualitative test for sulfate ions in analytical chemistry.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Instant reaction. BaSO₄ is used in medical imaging (barium meal) because of its insolubility.
safety_notes: Toxic — handle with extreme care.

observation_en: Magnesium ribbon dissolves rapidly with vigorous hydrogen bubbles and the solution warms noticeably.
explanation_en: Magnesium displaces hydrogen from HCl because Mg is above H in the reactivity series. Mg is oxidized to Mg²⁺ while H⁺ is reduced to H₂ gas.
condition_notes: Acidic medium. Room temperature. Reaction is fast with Mg ribbon, slower with Mg powder due to passivation.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: The iron nail becomes coated with a reddish-brown deposit of copper. The blue CuSO₄ solution fades to pale green (FeSO₄).
explanation_en: Iron is more reactive than copper. Fe atoms lose electrons (oxidized to Fe²⁺), while Cu²⁺ gains electrons (reduced to metallic Cu). This is a single displacement redox reaction.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Leave for 15–30 minutes for visible copper deposit. The blue→green color change confirms reaction progress.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: Potassium reacts violently with water, producing a lilac/purple flame as the hydrogen ignites spontaneously. Molten potassium skitters on the water surface.
explanation_en: Potassium is even more reactive than sodium (Group 1, Period 4). It instantly reduces water, releasing H₂ which ignites from the heat of reaction. The lilac flame is characteristic of potassium ions.
condition_notes: Instant at any temperature. Do NOT use pieces larger than a grain of rice. Stand behind a safety screen.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Lithium fizzes steadily on the water surface, shrinking as it reacts. Much gentler than Na or K. A crimson flame may appear if ignited.
explanation_en: Lithium (Group 1, lightest alkali metal) reacts with water to produce LiOH and H₂ gas. The reaction is less violent than sodium because Li has a higher ionization energy and smaller atomic radius.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Gentle steady reaction compared to Na and K. Good for comparing alkali metal reactivity trends.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Effervescence with CO₂ bubbles. The solution fizzes as the carbonate reacts with the acid.
explanation_en: Na₂CO₃ is a salt of a weak acid (H₂CO₃) and strong base (NaOH). HCl displaces the weak acid, which immediately decomposes to CO₂ and H₂O. The CO₂ evolved can be tested with limewater.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Classic carbonate identification test.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Calcium reacts moderately with water, producing hydrogen gas bubbles and a milky Ca(OH)₂ suspension (limewater).
explanation_en: Calcium (Group 2, alkaline earth) reacts with water more gently than alkali metals. Ca reduces water to form Ca(OH)₂ and H₂. The milky appearance is due to slightly soluble Ca(OH)₂.
condition_notes: Room temperature. The reaction rate is between Mg (very slow) and Na (violent). Good for comparing Group 2 vs Group 1 reactivity.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: The white ZnO powder dissolves in the acid to form a clear, colorless ZnCl₂ solution.
explanation_en: ZnO is an amphoteric oxide that acts as a base here, neutralizing HCl. The oxide ion accepts protons to form water, while Zn²⁺ and Cl⁻ remain in solution.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. ZnO dissolves slowly — stirring helps.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: The black CuO powder dissolves, and the solution turns blue as copper(II) sulfate forms.
explanation_en: CuO (a basic oxide) reacts with H₂SO₄ in a neutralization reaction. The Cu²⁺ ions in solution give the characteristic blue color of copper sulfate.
condition_notes: Gentle heating accelerates the reaction. Acidic medium. The blue color confirms Cu²⁺ formation.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: The solution heats up noticeably. No visible color change occurs, but the pH shifts from acidic/basic toward neutral.
explanation_en: A classic strong acid–strong base neutralization. H⁺ from H₂SO₄ combines with OH⁻ from NaOH to form water. Na₂SO₄ is a neutral salt that remains dissolved.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Exothermic. Monitor with pH indicator to observe neutralization point.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: KOH and HCl are both corrosive.

observation_en: Heat is released as the acid neutralizes the base. The resulting KCl solution is colorless.
explanation_en: KOH provides OH⁻ which combines with H⁺ from HCl. The resulting KCl is a neutral salt. This reaction has virtually the same enthalpy change as any strong acid–strong base neutralization (−57 kJ/mol).
condition_notes: Room temperature. Monitor pH to find the equivalence point.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: White MgO powder dissolves in the acid to produce a clear solution of MgCl₂. Slight warming occurs.
explanation_en: MgO is a basic oxide. The O²⁻ ion accepts protons from HCl to form water, while Mg²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions remain in solution. This is an example of oxide-acid neutralization.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium required. Stirring accelerates dissolution of MgO.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: The white powder releases steam and CO₂ gas upon strong heating. A residue of Na₂CO₃ remains.
explanation_en: NaHCO₃ is thermally unstable. Heating to 270°C breaks it into Na₂CO₃, H₂O, and CO₂. This is why baking soda causes baked goods to rise — CO₂ gas creates bubbles in the dough.
condition_notes: Requires strong heating (~270°C). The evolved CO₂ can be tested with limewater (turns milky).
safety_notes: Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Heating required — use heat-resistant glassware.

warnings_en: Ammonia gas has a pungent odor.

observation_en: A pungent ammonia smell is detected. Damp red litmus paper held over the tube turns blue, confirming NH₃.
explanation_en: NaOH (strong base) displaces the weak base NH₃ from its salt. NH₄⁺ + OH⁻ → NH₃↑ + H₂O. The ammonia gas escapes, driving the reaction forward. Classic test for ammonium salts.
condition_notes: Gentle warming speeds up NH₃ release. Basic medium. Use damp litmus paper to detect ammonia.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Copper deposits on the zinc surface as a reddish-brown coating. The blue solution fades to colorless as CuSO₄ is consumed.
explanation_en: Zinc is more reactive than copper. Zn is oxidized to Zn²⁺ (losing 2 electrons), while Cu²⁺ is reduced to metallic copper. The blue Cu²⁺ color disappears as copper plates out.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Leave for 10–20 minutes for visible results. Blue→colorless transition tracks reaction progress.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: Burns with a clean blue flame, producing heat, CO₂, and water vapor. Little visible soot if combustion is complete.
explanation_en: Ethanol undergoes complete combustion in excess oxygen: C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O. The C-H and C-C bonds are broken, releasing energy. Ethanol is used as a biofuel.
condition_notes: Requires ignition source (~363°C flash point). Burns cleanly in excess O₂. In limited O₂, produces CO or soot (incomplete combustion).
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: Can form explosive mixtures with air.

observation_en: Burns with a pale blue flame, producing CO₂ and H₂O. This is the reaction in natural gas burners.
explanation_en: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Methane combustion is highly exothermic (−890 kJ/mol). The C-H bonds break and reform as C=O and O-H bonds, which are lower in energy. This is the primary energy source in natural gas.
condition_notes: Requires ignition temperature (~537°C). Burns cleanly in excess oxygen. Natural gas is ~95% methane.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: CO₂ released in enclosed spaces is hazardous.

observation_en: Carbon glows orange-red as it burns. CO₂ is produced (invisible gas, turns limewater milky).
explanation_en: Solid carbon (charcoal) burns in oxygen to produce CO₂: C + O₂ → CO₂. ΔH = −394 kJ/mol. In limited oxygen, poisonous CO is formed instead (incomplete combustion).
condition_notes: Requires ignition (~400°C). Ensure excess oxygen for complete combustion. CO is produced in limited O₂ — dangerous.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: Burns in a fume hood ONLY.

observation_en: Sulfur melts and burns with a blue flame, producing SO₂ gas with a choking, pungent smell.
explanation_en: Sulfur burns in oxygen to produce sulfur dioxide: S + O₂ → SO₂. The characteristic blue flame and sharp odor are distinctive. SO₂ in the atmosphere leads to acid rain (H₂SO₃).
condition_notes: Ignition at ~232°C. MUST use fume hood — SO₂ is toxic. The blue flame is a key identifier.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required. Heating required — use heat-resistant glassware. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: White phosphorus ignites spontaneously in air!

observation_en: White phosphorus ignites with a bright white flame, producing dense white smoke (P₂O₅). Red phosphorus ignites at higher temperature.
explanation_en: Phosphorus is oxidized by oxygen: 4P + 5O₂ → 2P₂O₅. White phosphorus is highly reactive (autoignition ~44°C). P₂O₅ is a powerful desiccant that absorbs moisture from the air.
condition_notes: White phosphorus autoignites at ~44°C. Store under water. Use red phosphorus for safer classroom demonstrations.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: HNO₃ is a strong oxidizing acid.

observation_en: Heat is released. The resulting NaNO₃ solution is clear and colorless.
explanation_en: Strong acid–strong base neutralization: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O. NaNO₃ (sodium nitrate) is a soluble neutral salt used as fertilizer and in fireworks.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Exothermic. NaNO₃ product is a strong oxidizer — store carefully when dried.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: BaO dissolves exothermically in water, producing a strongly basic Ba(OH)₂ solution.
explanation_en: Barium oxide is a basic oxide. It reacts with water to form barium hydroxide, a strong and moderately soluble base. BaO + H₂O → Ba(OH)₂. Reaction is exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Exothermic. Ba(OH)₂ is toxic — handle with care.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Significant heat release — handle with caution.

observation_en: Vigorous exothermic reaction. The solution becomes strongly basic (NaOH formed).
explanation_en: Sodium oxide is a basic oxide: Na₂O + H₂O → 2NaOH. The ionic O²⁻ readily accepts protons from water. The resulting solution is strongly alkaline (pH ~14 for concentrated solutions).
condition_notes: Instant at room temperature. Very exothermic. Add small amounts of Na₂O to water.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Significant heat release — handle with caution.

observation_en: A rusty-brown gelatinous precipitate of Fe(OH)₃ forms immediately (decomposes to Fe₂O₃ on standing/heating).
explanation_en: Fe³⁺ ions combine with OH⁻ to form insoluble Fe(OH)₃, which appears as a rusty-brown precipitate. On standing or heating, it dehydrates to Fe₂O₃. This is a qualitative test for Fe³⁺ ions.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Basic medium stabilizes hydroxide. The color identifies iron(III) specifically — Fe(OH)₂ from Fe²⁺ would be green.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: The beaker becomes noticeably cold as NH₄NO₃ dissolves. Temperature can drop by 20–25°C. Used in instant cold packs.
explanation_en: Dissolving NH₄NO₃ in water is a strongly endothermic process (ΔH = +25.7 kJ/mol). The lattice energy of the solid is greater than the hydration energy of the ions, so energy is absorbed from surroundings.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Endothermic — temperature drops significantly. This is a physical dissolution, not a chemical reaction per se, but demonstrates energy changes.
safety_notes: Oxidizer — do not mix with combustibles. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Extreme cold — risk of frostbite on container.

observation_en: A pale cream-yellow precipitate of AgBr forms. AgBr is photosensitive and darkens on exposure to light.
explanation_en: Ag⁺ + Br⁻ → AgBr↓. Silver bromide is insoluble and precipitates. The cream color distinguishes it from white AgCl. AgBr was historically used in photographic film due to its light sensitivity.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Protect precipitate from light for accurate color observation. Compare with AgCl (white) and AgI (yellow) for halide identification.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: The orange dichromate solution turns green as Cr₂O₇²⁻ is reduced to Cr³⁺. A vinegar-like smell indicates acetic acid formation.
explanation_en: Dichromate (Cr⁶⁺, orange) is reduced to Cr³⁺ (green) while ethanol is oxidized first to acetaldehyde, then to acetic acid. This orange→green color change is used in breathalyzer tests.
condition_notes: Warm gently (~50°C). Acidic medium (H₂SO₄). The breathalyzer test relies on this exact color change to detect alcohol.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: Mg(OH)₂ is relatively safe (used in antacids).

observation_en: The white Mg(OH)₂ suspension slowly dissolves in the acid, forming a clear MgCl₂ solution.
explanation_en: Mg(OH)₂ (milk of magnesia) neutralizes stomach acid (HCl) in a base-acid reaction. This is how antacid tablets work — they contain Mg(OH)₂ or similar bases to reduce stomach acidity.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Mg(OH)₂ is a weak base but effective antacid. Stir for faster dissolution.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: SO₂ gas is toxic — use fume hood.

observation_en: The clear solution gradually becomes cloudy and then opaque yellow as colloidal sulfur forms. SO₂ smell is detected. Classic 'disappearing cross' experiment.
explanation_en: Na₂S₂O₃ + 2HCl → 2NaCl + S↓ + SO₂↑ + H₂O. The thiosulfate ion decomposes in acid. Colloidal sulfur causes the cloudiness. This reaction is widely used to study reaction rates (temperature vs. rate).
condition_notes: Room temperature. Rate increases with temperature — classic kinetics experiment. Time how long until a cross beneath the beaker disappears.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: Calcium hydroxide is caustic. Wear gloves.

observation_en: The milky lime water clears as the reaction proceeds. The solution warms slightly.
explanation_en: Calcium hydroxide provides two OH⁻ ions per formula unit. Each reacts with HCl to form water, while Ca²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions form soluble calcium chloride. The exothermic neutralization releases heat.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Use dilute HCl. Two moles HCl per mole Ca(OH)₂ for complete neutralization.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Sulfuric acid is corrosive. Always add acid to water.

observation_en: The solution heats up noticeably. No color change or precipitate. Clear solution of potassium sulfate remains.
explanation_en: Sulfuric acid is diprotic, donating two H⁺ ions. Each KOH neutralizes one proton. The resulting potassium sulfate is highly soluble. This is strongly exothermic due to the strong acid–strong base pairing.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Strongly exothermic. Add acid slowly to base solution.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Barium hydroxide is toxic if ingested.

observation_en: Clear solution forms with noticeable heat release. No visible precipitate.
explanation_en: Nitric acid donates H⁺ to OH⁻ from barium hydroxide, forming water. Ba²⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions remain dissolved as barium nitrate. This strong acid–strong base reaction is highly exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Handle barium compounds with care due to toxicity.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: LiOH is caustic. Handle with gloves.

observation_en: Solution warms slightly. Vinegar smell diminishes as acetic acid is consumed.
explanation_en: Acetic acid is a weak acid that partially donates H⁺ to OH⁻ from LiOH. The resulting lithium acetate is soluble. This weak acid–strong base neutralization is mildly exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Weak acid–strong base reaction produces a basic salt.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Phosphoric acid is corrosive at high concentration.

observation_en: Solution heats up as all three acidic protons are neutralized. Clear solution remains.
explanation_en: Phosphoric acid is triprotic, donating three H⁺ ions stepwise. Three NaOH molecules neutralize all protons, forming trisodium phosphate and water. Each neutralization step is exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Three equivalents of NaOH needed for complete neutralization.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: HCl fumes are irritating. Work in ventilated area.

observation_en: The white gelatinous aluminum hydroxide dissolves slowly as acid is added. Solution clears.
explanation_en: Aluminum hydroxide is amphoteric but dissolves in excess acid. Three HCl molecules provide H⁺ to neutralize each OH⁻ group, forming soluble aluminum chloride and water.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Al(OH)₃ dissolves slowly; stir well.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: Silver nitrate stains skin and clothing.

observation_en: A pale yellow precipitate of silver bromide (AgBr) forms immediately upon mixing.
explanation_en: Silver ions (Ag⁺) combine with bromide ions (Br⁻) to form insoluble silver bromide, which precipitates out. AgBr is light-sensitive and is the basis of photographic film chemistry.
condition_notes: Room temperature. AgBr is insoluble. Protect from light to prevent darkening.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Environmental hazard — dispose properly.

warnings_en: Avoid skin contact. Dispose of lead waste safely.

observation_en: A white precipitate of lead chloride (PbCl₂) forms. It redissolves in hot water.
explanation_en: Lead(II) ions combine with chloride ions to form lead(II) chloride (PbCl₂), which is sparingly soluble at room temperature. Unlike most chlorides, PbCl₂ precipitates due to its low Ksp.
condition_notes: Room temperature. PbCl₂ is more soluble in hot water. Handle lead compounds with extreme care.
safety_notes: Toxic — handle with extreme care. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk. Environmental hazard — dispose properly.

observation_en: A white cloudy precipitate of calcium carbonate forms immediately. The solution turns milky.
explanation_en: Calcium ions and carbonate ions combine to form insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), the same compound found in chalk, limestone, and marble. This is a classic double displacement reaction.
condition_notes: Room temperature. CaCO₃ is highly insoluble (Ksp ≈ 3.3 × 10⁻⁹).

warnings_en: Barium chloride is toxic if ingested.
BaSO₄ precipitate is safe once formed.

observation_en: A dense white precipitate of barium sulfate forms instantly. The precipitate is very fine and settles slowly.
explanation_en: Barium ions and sulfate ions form barium sulfate (BaSO₄), one of the most insoluble salts known (Ksp ≈ 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰). This reaction is used as a test for sulfate ions in qualitative analysis.
condition_notes: Room temperature. BaSO₄ is insoluble in water and dilute acids.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: Copper sulfate is harmful if ingested.

observation_en: A blue-green precipitate of basic copper carbonate forms. The blue solution turns cloudy and greenish.
explanation_en: Copper(II) ions react with carbonate ions to initially form CuCO₃, which in aqueous solution often becomes basic copper carbonate Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃, a green mineral similar to malachite.
condition_notes: Room temperature. The precipitate may appear green rather than blue due to basic carbonate formation.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Environmental hazard — dispose properly.

observation_en: A rusty-brown precipitate of iron(III) hydroxide forms with gentle fizzing as CO₂ is released.
explanation_en: Fe³⁺ ions react with carbonate and water to form insoluble Fe(OH)₃, which is a rusty brown gelatinous precipitate. CO₂ gas is released because Fe³⁺ is acidic enough to decompose the carbonate ion.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Both precipitation and gas evolution occur simultaneously.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Silver crystals grow on the copper surface as needle-like deposits. The colorless solution gradually turns blue as Cu²⁺ ions dissolve.
explanation_en: Copper is more reactive than silver (higher in the activity series). Cu atoms lose 2 electrons to become Cu²⁺ (blue solution), while 2 Ag⁺ ions each gain an electron to deposit as metallic silver on the copper surface.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Slow reaction; crystals grow over minutes. Copper must be in contact with the solution.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Copper chloride is harmful if ingested.

observation_en: Reddish copper metal deposits on the iron surface. The green-blue CuCl₂ solution turns brown-yellow as FeCl₃ forms.
explanation_en: Iron is more reactive than copper in the activity series. Fe is oxidized to Fe³⁺, donating electrons to Cu²⁺ ions which are reduced to metallic copper. This is a classic single displacement redox reaction.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Clean iron surface for best results. Solution color change confirms the reaction.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: Zinc is relatively safe but wash hands.

observation_en: Shiny lead crystals (lead tree) grow on the zinc surface. Beautiful dendritic patterns form.
explanation_en: Zinc is higher than lead in the activity series, so Zn atoms are oxidized to Zn²⁺ while Pb²⁺ ions are reduced to metallic lead. The lead deposits as beautiful crystalline dendrites known as Saturn’s tree.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Lead crystals grow slowly over several minutes. Handle lead waste carefully.
safety_notes: Toxic — handle with extreme care. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk.

warnings_en: Reaction is exothermic. Solution heats up.

observation_en: Copper deposits on the magnesium surface as a brown-red coating. The blue CuSO₄ solution fades to colorless MgSO₄. Noticeable heat.
explanation_en: Magnesium is much more reactive than copper. Mg loses 2 electrons to become Mg²⁺ while Cu²⁺ gains 2 electrons to become metallic copper. The large difference in reactivity makes this fast and exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Vigorous reaction due to large reactivity gap between Mg and Cu.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: Hydrogen gas is flammable. No open flames.

observation_en: Vigorous bubbling as hydrogen gas is produced. The zinc dissolves gradually. Solution warms.
explanation_en: Zinc is above hydrogen in the activity series, so it displaces hydrogen from sulfuric acid. Zn is oxidized to Zn²⁺ while H⁺ ions are reduced to H₂ gas. The reaction is exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Use dilute H₂SO₄. Keep away from flames due to H₂.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Slow steady bubbling as hydrogen evolves. The solution turns pale green as Fe²⁺ ions form.
explanation_en: Iron reacts with hydrochloric acid in a single displacement reaction. Fe atoms lose 2 electrons to become Fe²⁺ (green color), while 2 H⁺ ions gain electrons to form H₂ gas. Iron is above hydrogen in the reactivity series.
condition_notes: Slightly above room temperature optimal. Clean iron surface speeds reaction. Slower than zinc–acid reactions.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Initial fizzing that slows and stops. A white coating of calcium sulfate forms on the marble surface, blocking further reaction.
explanation_en: The acid reacts with CaCO₃ to release CO₂ gas. However, unlike HCl, H₂SO₄ produces insoluble CaSO₄ which coats the marble surface and eventually stops the reaction. This self-limiting behavior is unique to the sulfuric acid variant.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Reaction self-limits due to insoluble CaSO₄ coating. Compare with HCl + CaCO₃ which goes to completion.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Vigorous bubbling and significant heat generation. The aluminum dissolves rapidly. Solution may boil locally.
explanation_en: Aluminum is highly reactive and displaces hydrogen from HCl. Al loses 3 electrons per atom (Al → Al³⁺) while H⁺ ions gain electrons to form H₂ gas. The reaction is very exothermic due to aluminum’s high reactivity.
condition_notes: Needs slight warming to start (aluminum oxide layer). Once started, self-sustaining exothermic reaction.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: Burns with a nearly invisible flame.

observation_en: Burns with a pale blue, nearly invisible flame. Produces significant heat. CO₂ and water vapor released.
explanation_en: Methanol undergoes complete combustion in excess oxygen. Each CH₃OH molecule is oxidized: carbon goes from −2 to +4 oxidation state (in CO₂) and hydrogen forms water. The flame is nearly invisible, making methanol fires particularly dangerous.
condition_notes: Requires ignition temperature of ~385°C. Complete combustion requires excess O₂. Dangerous: invisible flame.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

observation_en: Brilliant white-hot flame with very high temperature. Produces intense light and heat. Sooty flame if O₂ is insufficient.
explanation_en: Acetylene has a high carbon-to-hydrogen ratio and a triple bond, making its combustion extremely exothermic (~1300 kJ/mol). The flame temperature reaches ~3300°C, hot enough to cut steel. Incomplete combustion produces soot (carbon particles).
condition_notes: Ignition at ~305°C. Very high flame temperature. Used in oxy-acetylene welding. Ensure adequate O₂ supply.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: Combustion produces CO₂ and water vapor.

observation_en: Glucose chars and caramelizes before burning. Sweet smell initially, then burning odor. In the body, enzymes catalyze this at 37°C.
explanation_en: This is the same net reaction as cellular respiration (C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O). In a flame, it occurs violently at high temperature. In living cells, enzymes lower the activation energy dramatically, allowing it at body temperature in controlled steps.
condition_notes: High ignition temperature (~410°C) without catalyst. With biological enzymes, occurs at 37°C. Classic comparison of combustion vs. respiration.
safety_notes: Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed. Open flame — fire extinguisher must be nearby.

warnings_en: Steam and SO₂ may be released.

observation_en: Dramatic carbon snake — the sugar turns black, expands upward as a column of carbon foam. Intense heat and steam released.
explanation_en: Concentrated sulfuric acid is a powerful dehydrating agent. It removes water (H and O atoms) from sucrose, leaving behind pure carbon. The heat generated turns the remaining water to steam, which expands the carbon into a dramatic porous column.
condition_notes: Room temperature start but highly exothermic. CONCENTRATED H₂SO₄ only. Dramatic demonstration reaction. Fume hood required.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: NO₂ gas is toxic brown gas.
Concentrated HNO₃ is a strong oxidizer.

observation_en: Copper dissolves producing a blue Cu(NO₃)₂ solution. Brown toxic NO₂ gas is evolved. Reaction is vigorous with concentrated acid.
explanation_en: Unlike HCl, nitric acid can dissolve copper because NO₃⁻ acts as an oxidizing agent. Cu is oxidized to Cu²⁺ (blue color) while NO₃⁻ is reduced to brown NO₂ gas. This demonstrates that HNO₃ is an oxidizing acid, unlike HCl.
condition_notes: Acidic medium. MUST use fume hood due to toxic NO₂. Dilute HNO₃ produces NO instead of NO₂.
safety_notes: Oxidizer — do not mix with combustibles. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Slow bubbling of hydrogen gas. Solution turns pale green as Ni²⁺ ions form. Nickel dissolves slowly.
explanation_en: Nickel is above hydrogen in the activity series but only slightly, so the reaction is slow. Ni is oxidized to Ni²⁺ (green color) while H⁺ is reduced to H₂ gas. Heating speeds the reaction significantly.
condition_notes: Needs gentle warming. Acidic medium. Reaction is slow due to nickel’s moderate reactivity.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Vigorous effervescence as CO₂ is released. The solid sodium carbonate dissolves rapidly.
explanation_en: Nitric acid reacts with sodium carbonate in a double displacement reaction. The carbonic acid intermediate (H₂CO₃) immediately decomposes into CO₂ gas and water. NaNO₃ remains dissolved.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Classic acid–carbonate reaction pattern.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Intense foaming and bubbling. More vigorous than vinegar + baking soda due to stronger acid.
explanation_en: Sulfuric acid, being diprotic, reacts with two NaHCO₃ molecules. Each bicarbonate produces one CO₂ molecule, resulting in double the gas volume. The reaction demonstrates acid strength affecting reaction vigor.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Acidic medium. Use dilute acid and large container to prevent overflow.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

observation_en: Solution turns dark brown as iodine (I₂) is liberated. Adding starch would turn it blue-black (iodine test).
explanation_en: Fe³⁺ is a stronger oxidizing agent than I₂. It oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ (brown color) while being reduced to Fe²⁺ (pale green). This is a redox reaction. The iodine–starch test confirms I₂ formation.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Can confirm I₂ with starch indicator (blue-black color).
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: AgNO₃ stains skin and causes burns.

observation_en: White curdy precipitate of AgCl forms immediately. The precipitate darkens to grey/purple when exposed to light.
explanation_en: Ag⁺ and Cl⁻ ions combine to form insoluble silver chloride (AgCl, Ksp ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰). This is a standard test for chloride ions in analytical chemistry. AgCl is photosensitive and decomposes to Ag metal in light.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Classic halide test. Protect AgCl from light to maintain white color.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: The black copper oxide powder dissolves in the acid. The solution turns blue-green as CuCl₂ forms. Mild warming.
explanation_en: Copper(II) oxide is a basic oxide that reacts with acids. The oxide ion acts as a base, accepting H⁺ from HCl. Cu²⁺ ions dissolve to form the blue-green copper chloride solution. This demonstrates the acidic reaction of a metal oxide.
condition_notes: Slight warming aids dissolution. Acidic medium. Classic base oxide + acid → salt + water.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: MgO dust is irritating to lungs.

observation_en: The white MgO powder slowly absorbs water and forms a white paste of Mg(OH)₂. Mild heat release.
explanation_en: Magnesium oxide reacts with water to form magnesium hydroxide, also known as milk of magnesia. The reaction is much less vigorous than CaO + H₂O because MgO has a higher lattice energy and is less reactive with water.
condition_notes: Slightly above room temperature. Slow reaction compared to CaO + H₂O. Mg(OH)₂ is only slightly soluble.

warnings_en: P₂O₅ is extremely hygroscopic and corrosive.

observation_en: Vigorous reaction with hissing. P₂O₅ dissolves rapidly generating significant heat. Solution becomes acidic.
explanation_en: Phosphorus pentoxide is one of the most powerful desiccants known. It reacts vigorously with water to form phosphoric acid. The P=O bonds in P₂O₅ break as water molecules insert, creating P–OH bonds in H₃PO₄.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Extremely exothermic. P₂O₅ is used as a desiccant precisely because of this affinity for water.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Significant heat release — handle with caution.

warnings_en: Forms sulfuric acid on contact with water or tissue.

observation_en: White fumes form as SO₃ contacts moisture. Extremely exothermic dissolution. The resulting solution is strongly acidic (sulfuric acid).
explanation_en: SO₃ dissolves in water to form sulfuric acid in a highly exothermic reaction. This is the final step in the industrial Contact Process for H₂SO₄ manufacture. In practice, SO₃ is absorbed into concentrated H₂SO₄ to form oleum, then diluted.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Extremely exothermic and corrosive. Industrial process uses oleum absorption instead of direct water contact.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Significant heat release — handle with caution.

warnings_en: SO₂ and SO₃ are toxic and corrosive.

observation_en: No visible change (gas to gas). The V₂O₅ catalyst glows orange-hot. SO₃ can be detected by its fuming in moist air.
explanation_en: This is the key step in the Contact Process for making sulfuric acid. V₂O₅ catalyst lowers the activation energy, allowing SO₂ oxidation at practical temperatures. The catalyst works by cycling between V⁵⁺ and V⁴⁺ oxidation states.
condition_notes: Requires ~450°C with V₂O₅ catalyst (or ~600°C without). Equilibrium favors SO₃ at lower temperatures but kinetics require higher temperatures — a classic compromise.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

warnings_en: NO₂ is highly toxic brown gas.

observation_en: The platinum gauze glows red-hot. Brown NO₂ fumes are produced. Ammonia smell is replaced by pungent acidic fumes.
explanation_en: The Ostwald Process converts ammonia to nitric acid via NO and then NO₂. Platinum catalyst enables selective oxidation to NO (which further oxidizes to NO₂) rather than direct oxidation to N₂ and H₂O. This is the industrial route to all nitrate-based fertilizers and explosives.
condition_notes: Requires Pt catalyst at ~750–850°C. Without catalyst, NH₃ simply burns to N₂ + H₂O. Multi-step industrial process.
safety_notes: Toxic — handle with extreme care. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

observation_en: The white zinc oxide powder dissolves in the acid. Solution clears. Mild warming.
explanation_en: ZnO is an amphoteric oxide that reacts with both acids and bases. With H₂SO₄, the oxide acts as a base, neutralizing the acid to form zinc sulfate and water. ZnO dissolves because the ionic Zn²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ are soluble.
condition_notes: Slight warming helps. Acidic medium. ZnO is amphoteric (also dissolves in NaOH).
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

warnings_en: Wear gloves and work in fume hood.

observation_en: The dark green Cr₂O₃ slowly dissolves. Solution turns dark green as CrCl₃ forms.
explanation_en: Chromium(III) oxide is amphoteric but dissolves in strong acids. The Cr³⁺ ions in solution give a characteristic dark green color as chromium(III) chloride. Heating is needed because Cr₂O₃ has a very stable crystal structure.
condition_notes: Needs heating (~60°C). Acidic medium. Cr₂O₃ is very refractory (hard to dissolve). Used as a green pigment.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Health hazard — prolonged exposure risk. Fume hood required.

observation_en: The dark yellow-brown FeCl₃ solution rapidly decolorizes. Dark iron particles deposit. Significant heat released.
explanation_en: Magnesium is much higher than iron in the activity series. Mg atoms lose 2 electrons each, reducing Fe³⁺ to metallic iron. The very large difference in reactivity makes this reaction vigorous and highly exothermic.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Very exothermic due to large reactivity gap. Clean Mg surface for best results.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: Brilliant white sparks and intense white-yellow light. Molten iron droplets form. Temperature exceeds 2500°C. Blinding flash.
explanation_en: The thermite reaction is one of the most exothermic reactions known. Aluminum reduces iron(III) oxide to molten iron while being oxidized to aluminum oxide. The reaction produces temperatures above 2500°C, sufficient to melt iron. Used for welding railroad tracks.
condition_notes: Very high ignition temperature (~900°C, typically using a magnesium ribbon). Once started, self-sustaining. Cannot be extinguished with water.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Extreme heat hazard — risk of burns. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

observation_en: Very vigorous bubbling and effervescence. Magnesium ribbon dissolves rapidly. Solution heats up significantly.
explanation_en: Magnesium is high in the reactivity series and reacts vigorously with dilute sulfuric acid. Mg is oxidized to Mg²⁺ while H⁺ is reduced to H₂ gas. The reaction is strongly exothermic and proceeds rapidly.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Very vigorous — add Mg slowly. H₂ pop test can be performed.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

name_en: Copper Sulfate and Ammonia (Complex Ion Formation)

warnings_en: Cu compounds are harmful if ingested.

observation_en: First a pale blue Cu(OH)₂ precipitate forms with a little NH₃, then with excess NH₃ it dissolves to give a deep royal blue solution of [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺.
explanation_en: Ammonia acts as a ligand, donating its lone pair to Cu²⁺ to form the tetraamminecopper(II) complex [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺. The deep blue color is diagnostic and much more intense than the pale blue of hydrated Cu²⁺. This demonstrates ligand field theory and d–d transitions.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Basic medium (excess NH₃). First add small amount of NH₃ to see Cu(OH)₂ precipitate, then add excess to dissolve it.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Fume hood required.

observation_en: Intense blood-red color appears instantly upon mixing. Even trace amounts of Fe³⁺ produce visible color. Classic test for iron(III) ions.
explanation_en: Fe³⁺ ions react with SCN⁻ to form the intensely colored [Fe(SCN)]²⁺ complex. The blood-red color is due to charge transfer between Fe³⁺ and SCN⁻. This reaction is so sensitive it can detect Fe³⁺ at parts-per-million concentrations.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Extremely sensitive test. The equilibrium can be shifted by adding more Fe³⁺ or SCN⁻ (Le Chatelier’s principle demonstration).
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

observation_en: Shiny reddish copper surface gradually darkens to black CuO when heated in air. The oxide layer forms progressively.
explanation_en: Copper reacts with oxygen when heated to form black copper(II) oxide. At lower temperatures, red Cu₂O (copper(I) oxide) forms first, then further oxidation produces black CuO. This is much slower than iron rusting because the CuO layer partially protects the underlying metal.
condition_notes: Requires heating (~200°C). Unlike iron rust, copper oxide is somewhat protective. Green patina on copper is a different process (reacting with CO₂ and moisture).
safety_notes: Heating required — use heat-resistant glassware.

observation_en: The black CuO powder gradually turns reddish-brown as metallic copper forms. The mixture glows when heated. CO₂ gas is released.
explanation_en: Carbon is a stronger reducing agent than copper at high temperatures. Carbon is oxidized to CO₂ while Cu²⁺ in CuO is reduced to metallic copper. This demonstrates the principle of smelting — extracting metals from their ores using carbon.
condition_notes: Requires heating to ~400°C. Classic school demonstration of metal extraction by reduction. Mix CuO and charcoal powder thoroughly.
safety_notes: Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

name_en: Carbon Reduction of Iron Oxide (Blast Furnace)
observation_en: At high temperature, the red-brown iron oxide converts to grey metallic iron. CO₂ gas is released. In a blast furnace, molten iron collects at the bottom.
explanation_en: This is the industrial process for iron production (simplified). Carbon (as coke) reduces iron(III) oxide to metallic iron. At blast furnace temperatures (~1500°C), the actual reducing agent is CO formed from C + CO₂. This process has been used for over 3000 years.
condition_notes: Requires very high temperature (~700°C in lab, ~1500°C in blast furnace). The foundation of the Iron Age and modern steel industry.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Significant heat release — handle with caution. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

warnings_en: H₂ gas is flammable and explosive.
Purge apparatus with H₂ before heating.

observation_en: Black CuO turns shiny pink-orange as metallic copper forms. Water droplets condense on cool surfaces downstream.
explanation_en: Hydrogen gas reduces copper(II) oxide to metallic copper while being oxidized to water. This is a clean reduction producing only water as a byproduct. H₂ is the reducing agent, gaining oxygen from CuO.
condition_notes: Requires ~300°C. CRITICAL: purge apparatus with H₂ before heating to avoid explosion of H₂–air mixture. Classic reduction demonstration.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

warnings_en: Causes deep tissue burns without immediate pain.

observation_en: No visible change. The solution warms slightly. HF is a weak acid despite its extreme danger to tissue.
explanation_en: HF is a weak acid (Ka = 6.8 × 10⁻⁴) due to the very strong H–F bond. However, it is extremely dangerous because fluoride ions penetrate tissue and bind calcium, causing deep burns and potentially fatal hypocalcemia. The neutralization with NaOH makes it safer.
condition_notes: Room temperature. HF is a weak acid but EXTREMELY hazardous. This reaction is used for safe disposal of HF waste.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care.

warnings_en: HBr is corrosive and produces irritating fumes.

observation_en: Clear solution remains. Noticeable heat release. No precipitate or gas evolution.
explanation_en: HBr is a strong acid that fully dissociates in water. It donates H⁺ to OH⁻ from KOH, forming water. K⁺ and Br⁻ ions remain dissolved as potassium bromide. KBr was historically used as a sedative in medicine.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Strong acid–strong base neutralization. Fully exothermic.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact.

warnings_en: Can cause fires with combustible materials.

observation_en: Purple crystals darken and melt, then release oxygen gas which can relight a glowing splint. Brown-black MnO₂ residue remains.
explanation_en: Potassium permanganate decomposes when heated, releasing oxygen gas. The Mn is reduced from +7 (in MnO₄⁻) to +4 (in MnO₂). The released O₂ can be tested with a glowing splint, which relights in the oxygen-rich atmosphere.
condition_notes: Needs heating to ~240°C. O₂ collection possible. The glowing splint test confirms oxygen evolution.
safety_notes: Oxidizer — do not mix with combustibles. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Heating required — use heat-resistant glassware.

warnings_en: Do not mix with combustible materials.

observation_en: White crystals melt and decompose. Oxygen gas released can relight a glowing splint. Residue is potassium nitrite (KNO₂).
explanation_en: Potassium nitrate decomposes on heating, with the nitrate ion (NO₃⁻) losing one oxygen atom to become nitrite (NO₂⁻). The released oxygen supports combustion. KNO₃ is saltpeter — the oxidizer in traditional gunpowder.
condition_notes: Requires ~400°C. KNO₃ is the oxidizer in fireworks and gunpowder. The oxygen-rich decomposition enables rapid combustion of fuels.
safety_notes: Oxidizer — do not mix with combustibles. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

observation_en: The white ZnO powder dissolves in the NaOH solution to form a clear zincate solution.
explanation_en: Zinc oxide is amphoteric, meaning it reacts with both acids and bases. With NaOH, ZnO acts as an acidic oxide, forming sodium zincate (Na₂ZnO₂). This dual behavior is characteristic of elements near the metal–nonmetal boundary.
condition_notes: Slightly above room temperature. Basic medium. Demonstrates amphoteric behavior of ZnO. Compare with ZnO + H₂SO₄.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection.

observation_en: Slow bubbling as hydrogen evolves. Tin dissolves gradually. Solution of tin(II) chloride forms.
explanation_en: Tin is above hydrogen in the activity series but only slightly, so the reaction is slow. Sn is oxidized to Sn²⁺ while H⁺ is reduced to H₂ gas. Heating accelerates the dissolution. Tin chloride (SnCl₂) is soluble.
condition_notes: Needs warming to ~50°C. Acidic medium. Slow reaction — compare with Zn + HCl which is much faster.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: Reaction can be vigorous when hot.

observation_en: Bubbling as hydrogen gas evolves. Aluminum foil dissolves. Solution may foam. Reaction speeds up as it heats itself.
explanation_en: Unlike most metals, aluminum reacts with strong bases as well as acids (amphoteric behavior). NaOH dissolves the protective Al₂O₃ layer, then Al reduces water to H₂ while being oxidized to aluminate ions [Al(OH)₄]⁻. Never clean aluminum with oven cleaner (NaOH-based).
condition_notes: Needs warming to ~50°C to start. Basic medium. Self-accelerating as exothermic. Demonstrates amphoteric nature of aluminum.
safety_notes: Flammable — keep away from ignition sources. Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: SiO₂ dust is hazardous to lungs.

observation_en: SiO₂ slowly dissolves in concentrated NaOH at high temperature, forming a clear sodium silicate solution (water glass).
explanation_en: SiO₂ is an acidic oxide with an extremely stable covalent network structure. It reacts with strong bases at high temperatures to form sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃, known as water glass). This is why NaOH is stored in plastic, not glass bottles.
condition_notes: Requires very high temperature or prolonged heating with concentrated NaOH. This is why NaOH etches glass and must be stored in plastic containers.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. High ignition temperature — specialized equipment needed.

observation_en: A bright yellow precipitate of silver iodide (AgI) forms immediately. Very distinctive canary yellow color.
explanation_en: Ag⁺ combines with I⁻ to form AgI, which is even less soluble than AgCl or AgBr (Ksp ≈ 8.5 × 10⁻¹⁷). The yellow color distinguishes it from white AgCl and pale yellow AgBr. AgI is used in cloud seeding to promote rain formation.
condition_notes: Room temperature. AgI is the least soluble silver halide. Color comparison: AgCl (white) < AgBr (pale yellow) < AgI (bright yellow).
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Environmental hazard — dispose properly.

observation_en: With catalyst (KI or MnO₂), rapid foaming and oxygen evolution. With KI + dish soap, creates dramatic elephant toothpaste foam column.
explanation_en: H₂O₂ is thermodynamically unstable and decomposes to water and oxygen. Without catalyst, this is extremely slow. KI or MnO₂ catalysts dramatically speed the decomposition. The elephant toothpaste demo adds dish soap to trap O₂ in foam, creating a spectacular eruption.
condition_notes: Room temperature with catalyst. Without catalyst, very slow. KI or MnO₂ as catalyst. Add dish soap and food coloring for the famous demonstration.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation.

warnings_en: CO₂ in high concentration displaces oxygen.
H₂CO₃ is a weak acid, safe.

observation_en: CO₂ dissolves in water with slight fizzing. The resulting solution is slightly acidic (pH ~5.6). This is what makes carbonated water.
explanation_en: CO₂ is an acidic oxide that dissolves in water to form carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a weak diprotic acid. Only a small fraction of dissolved CO₂ actually forms H₂CO₃ (about 0.3%). This reaction is responsible for the acidity of rainwater and carbonated beverages.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Higher pressure increases CO₂ solubility (carbonation). This is why rain is naturally slightly acidic (pH ~5.6).

observation_en: The colorless KBr solution turns orange-brown as bromine (Br₂) is liberated by the more reactive chlorine.
explanation_en: Chlorine is more electronegative than bromine and a stronger oxidizing agent. Cl₂ displaces Br⁻ from KBr solution, oxidizing Br⁻ to Br₂ (orange-brown) while Cl₂ is reduced to Cl⁻. This demonstrates halogen reactivity order: F > Cl > Br > I.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Classic halogen displacement series. Add organic solvent to extract Br₂ layer for clearer color observation.
safety_notes: Toxic — handle with extreme care. Environmental hazard — dispose properly. Fume hood required.

observation_en: The orange bromine solution decolorizes as brown iodine appears. Adding starch indicator turns the solution blue-black (iodine test).
explanation_en: Bromine is more reactive than iodine in the halogen series. Br₂ oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ while being reduced to Br⁻. The iodine can be confirmed with starch indicator (blue-black color). This completes the halogen displacement series.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Continue the halogen displacement pattern: Cl₂ displaces Br⁻, Br₂ displaces I⁻. Use starch to confirm I₂.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Toxic — handle with extreme care. Fume hood required.

warnings_en: NH₃ gas is irritating and pungent.

observation_en: The mixture becomes very cold (can freeze water on the outside of the beaker). Strong ammonia smell. The beaker frosts over.
explanation_en: This is one of the most dramatic endothermic reactions. Mixing two solids absorbs so much heat that the temperature drops to about −20°C. The reaction produces ammonia gas (pungent smell) and barium chloride. The extreme cooling can freeze a wet board to the beaker.
condition_notes: Room temperature. Extremely endothermic. Temperature drop of ~50°C possible. Classic demonstration of endothermic process.
safety_notes: Corrosive — wear gloves and eye protection. Irritant — avoid skin and eye contact. Extreme cold — risk of frostbite on container. Gas evolved — ensure adequate ventilation. Fume hood required.
